that he lost his self-possession. The very sound of the

aherdaniel is close to Derrynane, and the name of
Derrynane is closely associated with the memory of the
Liberator. There was a concert held at Caherdaniel
on Sunday, August 13th, and the arrangement of it
was largely, if not wholly, in the hands of the descen-
dants of O’Connell now residing at Derrynane House.
The children of the two schools in the neighbourhood
were asked to sing, but were requested not to sing an
frish song. A well-known Irish Ireland priest was
honoured by an invitation from the same quarter to
contribute a song to the entertainment, but not to sing
in Irish. Evidently the organisers were quite oblivious
of the significance of what they were doing. Sorme Irish
people so thoroughly despise Ireland that they are not
even conscious that they despise it.

The sway which prejudice holds over the minds of
some anti-Irish people may sometimes be very clearly
observed when these people turn their minds to
problems similar to those that present themselves in
Ireland. We all know what the “ Zrssh’’ T'imes, the
libeller of Canon M‘Inerney, feels with regard to the
Language Revival. For the blind partisan feels, and
does not think. The “ 7rish *’ Times recently set itself
national languages, and some people
may be surprised to hear that we agree very much with
the thoughts of the anti-Irish gloater over the proposed
Redistribution robbery.  There is nothing to be sur-
prised at, for the Zrush 7'imes made an offer at clear
thinking untrammelled by the force of blind prejudice.
The new effort at a sort of universal language sent the
Irish Tomes thinking on the nature and significance of
national languages. For the moment the libeller of
Canon MTnerney forgot all. about its bogus Roman
Catholic Farmer,”’ the Freemasons, the Pervert Priests’
Protectors, the Arnotts, the Irish Party, the Garvison,
and so its head worked freely. The libeller of Canon
M‘Incrney, in a thoughtful moment when it was off its
guard, and was oblivious of the Arnotts, wrote:—“ We
do not believe that any artificial language can ever
claim cquality with any national language, or pretend
to the same potency over the hearts and intellects of
those who employ it. Every national tongue contains
in its nature and history the ideas, emotions, and in-
spiration of the people who made it, and whom it has
made.””” We might have written that ourselves. We
are sure the Arnott family got a shock when they read
that at their breakfast one morning. Here is some
more :— If all languages were mere agglomerations of
words, Esperanto, by its convenience and simplicity,
would have a right to supplant many, or all of them;
but a national language is a much more complex thing.

_ :

.’a}')irit_ of the nation; every word of it is inextricably
-associated with national associations and traditions.
“Through this native medium all the best thoughts of a
nation will always confinue to be expressed.” That,
if anything, is worse, and is calculated to send Sir
John Arnott tearing his hair. Why, such sentiments
are almost as aggravating to a sterling England’s
Faithful Garrison_man as when Sir John discovered
that the bogus “ Roman Catholic Farmer '’ was not at
home even to a registered letter.

But there is more. The 7rish T'imes goes on:—
““Esperanto will record great national work for the
benefit of all the nations, but may never hope to inspire
it. For this reason, we are pretty sure that it will
mever be ‘ universal ’ in the highest sense, and that it
<an never be half so effective as national language in
_promoting such great and subtle cnterprises as the
spread of Christianity or the advancement of moral
civilisation.” Now to measure the force of anti-Inish
prejudice you need only compare the views expressed
on the reality and nature of national languages by the
Irish Times, when, in an unguarded moment, it forgot
all about the Arnotts and the Garrison—compare these
views with the views that it would express if it were
contemplating not the nature and reality of national
languages in general, but of the Irish national lan-
-guage in particular. The difference would fairly re-
present the force of anti-Irish prejudice.

It is interesting to turn one of the quotations we
‘have made from its general application to the particu-
lar case of Ireland. If for the general term “a na-
tional language ’’ we substitute “the Irish national
language, we would have the Z7ish Times writing as
follows : —“ But the Irish national language is a much
more complex thing. It has been modified and shaped
by the national literature; it reflects that very real
and distinct thing, the spirit of the nation ; every word
of it is inextricably associated with national associa-
tions and traditions. Through this native medium all
the best thoughts of a nation will always continue to
be expressed.”” Were it not about time that Sir John
talked to the editor for his own good?

We are glad to learn that the Irish brown Cavendish
“tobacco, manufactured by Messrs. Murray, Sons & Co.,
Ltd., Belfast, has gone off remarkably well. The leaf
from which this tobacco has been manufactured was
grown in Ireland, and the demand for it has heen so
considerable that s. Murray’s stock of leaf is ex-
pected to be exhausted in a few days. That speaks well
for the support accorded by the consumer to this new
development of an important Irish industry, and what
is perhaps more significant, as patriotic sentiment alone
will not permanently popularise an article, it also
speaks well for the quality of the Irish grown leaf. The
tobacco which Messrs. Murray have manufactured from
the Irish grown leaf sells refail at 44d. an ounce. In
corder to have an opinion at first hand we asked a
smoker to test some of it for us and he speaks in very
high terms of the result. Of course, we cannot expect
:that all the raw material used in Irish manufacture can
.be obtained in Ircland. Many of the most successful
industries have to look abroad for their raw material,
but is gratifying to find Irish firms like Messrs.
Murray’s when opportunity offers making use of it, and
o tending to expand the sphere of employment which
their industry creates.

Rathmines and Harold’s Cross districts have heen
plentifully supplied with objection notice papers dated
16th August.  ““ Idolators’’ are getting them of course
and are naturally indignant, for many of them at least
have occupied their present abodes for years past. The
objection papers are signed Thomas T. Ladd, 103 St.
Lawrence Road, Clontarf, Dublin.

Industrial Scction was again one of the features and
comprised a large and representative collection of Irish
products. The limited extent of the room devoted to
it was a bit of a drawback, but: one cannot have every-
thing one wants in this world, and this disadvantage
was compensated for as far as possible by an economic
distribution of the available space. The Dripsey
Woollen Co. had a fine exhibit of their costume cloths,
which was carly bought up in its entirety by Arnott
& Co. Dublin-made wicker chairs were shown by
Mossrs. Holohan. This collection, which has been
purchased by Todd Burns & C}')., Ltd., n_'ncludcd
two handsome fancy chairs of designs embodying the
shiolds of Leinster and Ulster. ~ Messrs. J. Barring-
ton & Soms had a nice exhibit of their well-known
soaps. Mineral waters and hop bitters, manufactured
by Messrs. John Egan & Sons, Dublin, were l:hcre in
abundance. Messrs. Egan also showed on their stand
a silver cup which they are presenting to the Con-
naught Provincial Committee of the G.A.A. as a prize
for hurling competitions.  Murphy’s starch, Dixon’s
soaps, lace work from Benada Abbey, Tubercurry and
Toxford cloths were also represented.  Gill’s, Fallon’s,
Blackie’s, and the Dun Emer Press showed various pub-
lications, and some handsome specimens of Messrs. John
O’Neill’s Lucania cycles were in evidence Messrs.
Hopkins and Hopkins had a fine exhibit of silver work,
cte. Two pretty children, a boy and girl,
dressed  respectively as a leprechaun and cai-
lin, attracted much attention at the stand of Messrs.
McMenamin Bros., which contained an extensive dis-
play of boot blacking polishes and kindred articles
which they manufacture at Rathfarnham. St. Tta’s
Factory, Limerick, had a neat exhibit of shirts, and
the Lough Glynn industry showed various examples of
lace-work, etc. The industry at North William Street,
Dublin, had two stands for artificial flowers and hosiery
respectively. Another blacking industry was also re-
presented “in the stand of Messrs. Malone Bros., of
Great Strand Street, on which were stocked boot
polishes, metal polishes, and other articles of their
manufacture. There are now two blacking manufac-
turers in Dublin, but enquiries at some Dublin retail
shops might lead one to think there were none. A lady
informs us that as late as Saturday last, on which day
the two manufacturing firms were showing in the
Rotunda, she asked at two shops in the south side of
Dublin for Irish-made boot polish. At one of them she
was not only informed that they did not stock it, but
leswoman launched into praises of the superior
of the British-made article, and was no doubt
somewhat astonished to be informed by the would-be
purchaser that it was bad enough for her not to have
Irish polish for sale, but it was too bad for her to go
out of her way to decry it, particularly as her shop was
thriving on Irish custom. At the second shop in the
south side they were sorry they hadn’t got it. Finally,
the lady got what she wanted in Drumcondra. The In-
dustrial 1ixhibition at the Rotunda does not close until
the end of this week. Some of these ‘‘ pufi-the-British-
article ”” shopkeepers might indeed pay a visit to it
with profit.

The entertainment side of the Oireachtas drew large
audiences. Mrs. Clandillon, formerly Miss Hannigan,
sang as one always oxpects her to sing. We have heard
it said of Mr. O’Brien’s speeches that his listeners
cannot but feel impressed with their ring of enthusiasm.
In Mys. Clandillon’s rendering of her native Irish songs
there is a note of feeling which adds to the effectiveness
of her fine voice. Irish Ireland has produced no better
singers than Messrs. Hallissey and Clandillon, and the
Oireachtas committee lost nothing in substituting them
for Scotch singers.  Miss Barry’s songs won merited
applause. Miss S. Hallissey also sang, but we missed
hearing her.  The appearance of Dr. O’Donnell
at Thursday night’s proceedings evoked a hearty wel-
come, to which his Lordship responded in a brief Irish
speech. The applause which punctuated Mr. Weldon’s
ode and Professor Macken’s Irish address showed the
great number present in he big attendance who were
capablo of appreciating them.

the twenbighh century, and even the nineteenth
was  associated  with parliaments open to the

Gaelic League, but, it is rumoured, the branch d g

Apparently the motion on the agenda having been with-

. drawn, the matter dvopped—and that was tho rather

The election of an Executive was carried out on the
one ward system—Rathmines, be it remembered, has
two wards. The election resulted as naturally a one-
ward system would result. Most of the old members
that were originally elected or co-opted were re-elected
just as outgoing directors on the Great Sourface Rail-
way are re-elected. Our suggestion that those seeking
election should let us know their views—the self-same
suggestion that Mr. Gore and Mr. Sullivan made with
regard to the proposed re-election of Ebenezer, George,
and William as Great Sourface directors—that sugges-
tion was not heeded. The meetings of the Executive
being private, the public and even the delegates to the
private parliament for the most part know nothing
practically of the merits or demerits of the members.

Fifteen new non-resident members were added to the
Executive, and that brought in some welcome fresh
blood. Fourteen of the first fifteen of the non-resident
members were on the previous machine—for the one-
ward machine works fairly reliably. The highest vote for
the election of the non-resident part of the machine was
176 ; one of the new men—a towering giant in the Irish
Ireland world—Mr. P. J. O’Shea, better known by his
pen name Condn Maot scrambled on to the machine
with a paltry vote of 93—no less than 83 below the top
vote of 176.  Mr. John O’Keane, now of Belfast, whose
work at Ballingeary our contributor ‘‘ Chanel *’ recently
referred to in an article, got on with a vote of 75,
that is, 101 below the highest. ~ Mr. M‘Donagh Ma-
hony, who has worked so cffectively in Iveragh, and
who, by the way, lost his position as magistrate in con-
sequence of his sturdy fight for the language, barely re-
tained his seat with a vote of 85, and Mr. Patrick
M‘Sweeny, of Fermoy, only barely retained his seat
also with a vote of 87. One of the best and most
earnest workers in the movement for many years, Mr.
Richard Foley, was knocked off, and Father Dinneen,
one of the greatest pillars of the Language )\Iovemcuh,
only received 124 votes. These facts show that strenu-
ous work does not necessarily carry most of the votes.

This college also offers scholarships. ~In the same
county at Cappoquin is the Mount Melleray Seminary,

practical.”’ = The Franciscan Brothers conduct another

trained for farming pursuits. St. Joseph’s College,
Ballinasloe, offers several scholarships value £220 oper.

Trish girls also have such a varied number of schools
to choose from that it is inexcusable that any ci them
should now he sent across the water. To go no further
that those that announce themselves in the LEADER
there are in Dublin St. Mary’s University College and
High School, Marlborough Road, conducted by the
Dominican Nuns. Special attention is given here toe
Irish, and it will be remembered that it was a student
from St. Mary’s who won an Irish studentship of the
R.U.I. They play camoguroeacc at St. Mary’s.
Loreto College, Stephen’s Green, Dublin, at the recent
R.U.I. examinations secured 31 Honours as against
22 by the Queen’s College, Belfast. In Dublin also
the Sisters of the Holy Faith condut a boarding school
for young ladies at Glasnevin. The convent of St.
Louis, Carrickmacross, had some time ago to add a new
wing to accommodate the increasing numbers. There
is also a convent of St. Louis at Ramsgrange, Wexford.
The Dominican College, Taylor’s Hill, Galway, and the
Convent of Mercy, St. Brigid’s School, Tuam, belong to
the extreme west. In Waterford there are St. Mary’s,
Ursuline Convent and the Residential School of Domes-
tic Science and Housekeeping for young ladies which
is carried on by the Ursuline Nuns also.

So varied is the choice to select fi-a in the case of
boys or of girls that there is no excuse for exporting any
students. Tf parents wish their childrca to be brought
up in an Irish direction several colleges and convents
are closely identified with the Irish larzuage effort;
there are, alas, even still institutions wh.re those who
feel hurt by Irish will not have their susceptibilities
outraged! There is, as far as we can see, no excuse for
any Irish parent sending his son or daughter to Eng-
land for schooling. Ireland offers plenty of oppor-
tunities, and it is reasonable in the last degree to ask
that every Irish boy and girl who goes to college should
be sent to collz ;o in Ireland in preference to England.

lection. The circular sets out that “ Having learned
that Head-Corstable Hanna is about leaving Kilbeg~

TRADITIONAL SINGING AND GAELIC LEAGUE CHOIRS. 
26 Fontenoy Street, Dubl1n. 
,o. CA 1' <,,~The question of traditional singing has 
been discussed many times, but up to the present no 
satisfactory definition of what it really is has been 
given. 
I have failed to elicit from those who are sup-
posed to be the true exponents of t'his style where the 
difference lies between the peasant who sings ,vith abso-
' lute disregard for the sentiment of the song and with-
out accompaniment, and the city singer who, anxious 
to keep within the strict lines of the Irish Ireland pro-
gramme, sings exactly the same song with the addition 
of light and· shade, phrasing, time and tune, etc, which 
he is accustomed to associate with the proper interpre-
tation of a song. 
The former is hailed by the few as 
the proper exponent of Irish singing while the latter is 
generally sneered at for his pains. 
If the so-called 
traditional singers could frame some standard or rule 
to be foJlowed, there might be some reason for this in-
tole_rant treatment of purely city singers, but as the 
former cannot even agree amongst themselves as to who 
has the true traditional style, I think it is time that 
some standard was adopted to settle this vexatious ques-
tion, or else that in the various foiseanna there should 
be separate competitions for traditional singing as apart 
from the modern interpretation of Irish melodies. 
As one who takes a keen interest in the development 
of the musical side of the league, religiously atte1~ds 
Irish concerts, and who moves largely amongst city 
workers in the cause I observe that in almost all cases 
the traditional singe~· after some time, beg~ns to rec~g-
nise that his peculiar style does not '.1-dapt i~self to city 
environment, and so we witness this smger ~nd that 
gradually introducing a pianoforte accompamment ~o 
bis or her song, revealing the remarkable fact tl~at their 
melodies a.re -frequently sung out of tune and time and 
exhibit a total absence of fooling or attempt to convey 
the sentiment of the song .as writt(l];i. 
. Now the city Leaguer is or was accustomed from e.arly 
years to hearing the best musical works with all the rich 
accompaniment of orchestral music in the concert hall 
or theatre, and he also knew that the streets on a Satur-
day night echoed to what he mistakenly called " Come 
a.llyees " given out by itinerant musicians. 
~le is told 
on joiiiing £he League that the traditional singing is 
the proper interpretation of the national music, and 
naturally enough from -the samples of the same he~rd 
at the Gaelic League concert, he associates them w~th , 
"come allyeos " when this fact is proclaimed to him -
Nevertheless he tries to bridge over the chasm, and r~-
cognising the influence of music as a valuable ally m 
the good cause, he urges his fellows to form a choir in 
his branch, so that tho untaught public may be gradu-
ally accustomed to hear in pleasant harmony the an-
cient music and become familiar with it. The conduc-
tor of his choir turns to the Gaelic League Offices and 
selects various traditional songs, arranged for two or 
four voices, which with an expenditure of great labour 
and time he successfully teaches his choir. 
A concert 
is given, and the curious hearing the harmony join the 
branch, and through the medium of the choir are put 
on the right road to the goal- an Irish Ireland. 
In Dublin numbers of fine workers have been brought 
bv this means into tho Lea<>ue. 
Similar cases I doubt 
n~t occur in other cities a; well.

THE RATHMINES DEBACLE.

OUR readers may have missed the significance,
and all it imports, of the little comedy enacted
in the closing hours of the recent Session of Parliament.
A sort of guerrilla struggle had been in progress between
the representatives of that close corporation known as
the Rathmines Urban Commissioners and a few vigilant,
representatives of the people. The bone of contention
was the private bill promoted by the former body for
the annexation of the Terenure district and for various
other purposes which would need careful investigation.
In the merits of this particular annexation T am not
deeply interested, except in so far as it secks to hand
over a large extent of territory at present under
popular government to an exclusive body which spends
the ratepayers’ money, without scruple, to flout popular
sentiment, and maintain an archaic system of represen-
tation, or rather misrepresentation.

Aided by a friendly Government which maintains in
Ireland a Chief Secretary to the Orangemen, the
sponsors for the bill were within sight of victory and
needed only a little resource and a little intelligence to
gain the day. But, alas, the call on the party of all
intelligence, etc., was too much, and so with the flicker-
ing life of Parliament passed away the last chance of
the bill—for the present.

The moral is obvious. Bodies which defy popular
opinion, and model themselves on Cromwellian prin-
ciples may, when they appeal to Parliament, expect to
be fought inch by inch without ruth. IHere at least the
people, although they may not be fit to elect a parish
beadle, may not only influence the fate of empires, but
may even stand up to such mighty personages as the
Rathmines Commissioners.

The bill will doubtless be heard of again. Financial
necessity will, T believe, render this imperative.  The
Commissioners may take warning from what has hap-
pened and prepare themselves for what is to come. I
believe too much latitude was allowed them this year,
and if they again approach the contest in the same
spirit they will learn the difference between an affair of
outposts and a general engagement along the whole line.
This corporate body is probably no worse than many
others which have long since been swept away, and
many sing may be forgiven them if they gracefully give
way before the march of progress and enlightenment.
Rip Van Winkle may rub his eyes and look round on a
world of which he accidentally forms an item, but which
has long since outstripped his ideas. The Gael, too,

‘has awakened from his long slumber and is about to

resume his inheritance. Never again will the Crom-
wellian be allowed to ride roughshod over the people’s
necks without his progress being stubbornly disputed.
The Rathmines Commissioners may note (zh.cso‘ facts or
they may not. Their bill will come on again in a new
House of Commons with a different government in
power. There will be no Orangemen’s Chief Secretary
then to call on. The deadheads will find their work cut

out for them. Now is the time to look for recruits. .

ask the Solicitor-General to exchange his opinion gzr a

The Letters Patent of 1667 changed the religious

The religious character impressed on the endowment

refused to conform to Episcopacy, and were expelled

Henry Jones, and the other six Trustees appointed by

is. that there’s not enough of it. I could do with a
separate article on “ The Gaelic League and Politics.”
The other articles leave you so convinced that politics
has scarcely anything to do with nationality, that little
happenings here and there in the Gaelic League—
leaguers being carried away by the old political spirit—
vex one. Another fault in the eyes of the many who
unconsciously and naturally have come to look to the
simple peasant—the simpler the better—for the reali-
zation of an Irish Ireland, may be that, in parts, the
articles are too deep. Well, they were written for
thinkers. Mmandar.

“Thomas O’Concannon is responsible for more than
the mere translation of the play. Ile it was who not
merely directed my attention to the theme, but laid
down " the lines on which it should run. The bones of
the play, I should say, are O’Concannon.” This, no
doubt, is the bare truth; but by the body of the play
it shall be judged by most people, I fancy, and the Eng-
lish body, at any rate, is the composition of Seumas
MacManus.

MacManus is an erratic genius. Mitchel makes use
of a very cffective simile about O’Connell, when he
compares him to the oak-tree of Virgil, whose roots
went down to Hades, while its branches soared aloft
to the heavens. It might be extravagant to apply
such language to Seumas MacManus, but really one is
tempted to do so, for, verily, at times he is almost sub-
lime, and at times despicable. It is rather a common
woakness with Irish writers to be uneven in the quality
of their work; but in MacManus this failing is de-
veloped to an abnormal extent. At his best, in his
deep pathos, his rich, rollicking humour, his broad,
human sympathies, his kindly delineation of character,
and, above all, in his wonderfully minute knowledge
of TIrish peasant life, he is unrivalled; at his worst, in
his strained attempts at wit, his ill-conceived plots and
feeble denouements, his bathos, buffoonery and mean
‘“stage Irishmanism,”” it were hard to find ‘him a
fitting compeer. I happen to have beside me, as I
write, a stray copy of his now happily extinet “ Irish
Nights.”” It is No. 2 of the series; and in the two
stories that it contains,  Billy Baxter’’ and “ The
Sorrow of Phelim Ruadh,”” this unevenness of Mac-
Manus is displayed to the fullest. For the latter tale
is one of the most beautiful, tenderest, and most
pathetic compositions that Anglo-Irish literature has
ever known, and perhaps, the truest to Irish life;
whilst the former is as low a travesty of our people’s
manners, and as loathsome a piece of “stage-Trish ”’
buffoonery as we have ever been treated to. It almost
staggers as when we attempt to realise that it was the
same man who conceived that most beautiful and heart-
rending of pictures, Phelim Ruadh sitting “ the len’th
of a lee-long winter’s night at the farthest end of the
fire from Neil’s books with the hunger for them in his
eye an’ in his heart,” as he who painted that other
picture, Billy Baxter sitting at Mrs. MacArthur’s table
in his shirt sleeves, and advising her guests to “dail
lightly ; take my word for it, and only take of these
what’ll fill the far lands!"

Fortunately, MacManus is at his best in “ The Haxd-
hearted Man.”” It is a real play with verve and cha-
racter and truth in it, and puts its author in quite a
new light before us—that of a first-class dramatist.
His best qualities are displayed to the fullest in it,
more especially his knowledge of, and sympathy with,
the people about whom he writes, and his kindly ten-
derness.  The play is ostensibly propagandist in its
conception, a fact which renders its success all the more
praiseworthy, when we consider how very difficult it is
to write good didactic literature. It is directed
against three of the great evils from which we suffer—
an unnational and unnatural system of education,
want of sincerity amongst ourselves, and emigration.
The first two of theso are dealt with merely incident-
ally, but both very successfully, the last is fought all
through the play, and may be said almost to furnish
the plot. 4 >

Maurice Ruddy, “the hard-hearted man,’’ is really
a powoerful piece of character-drawing; and there is
something exquisitely true and pathetic in this splen-
did picture of the brave, noble man, with lofty in-
stincts and high ideals, whom wrong and the spectacle

of national degradation and slavish meanness have so
embitbered and rendered fiercely. wrathful. The out-
Pourings of Maurice’s soul are, at times, strikingly
eloquent, and in these passages there is littlo to choose,
